extra.reasoning: This is a state machine. State vars: opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks (int 0-2), init opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0. Property: globally (opted_out implies not sent), i.e. no reachable state with opted_out=true AND sent=true. The only transition that can set sent:=true while opted_out is true is t5: 'on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 2): sent := true'. (t2 requires not opted_out; t3 sets sent:=false.) So if we can reach opted_out=true with ticks>=2 and then fire send_attempt taking t5, the property breaks. Trace: (1) optout -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0 [t1]; (2) tick -> ticks=1, sent=false [t4]; (3) tick -> ticks=2, sent=false [t4]; (4) send_attempt: guards of t3 (opted_out) and t5 (opted_out and ticks>=2) both hold; t5 fires and sets sent:=true, yielding opted_out=true AND sent=true -> VIOLATION. This is 4 events, within the 6-event bound. The design flaw is that t5 explicitly permits sending to an opted-out subject once ticks reaches the cap, directly contradicting the intended invariant; since the property must hold on every reachable execution, the existence of this execution (the machine can take t5) violates it.
